questions.q: Mostow rigidity asserts that for closed hyperbolic $n$-manifolds $M_1,M_2$ with $n\ge 3$, an isomorphism $\pi_1(M_1)\cong\pi_1(M_2)$ implies:
choices: $M_1$ and $M_2$ are isometric (and the isometry is unique up to homotopy)
$M_1$ and $M_2$ have the same fundamental group but possibly different hyperbolic metrics
questions.explain: Mostow upgrades a $\pi_1$-isomorphism all the way to a unique isometry — the hyperbolic metric is determined by the topology.

questions.q: Why does the theorem fail in dimension $n=2$?
choices: Closed hyperbolic surfaces of genus $g\ge 2$ form a $(6g-6)$-dimensional Teichmüller moduli space, so the metric is not unique
questions.explain: Teichmüller space has positive dimension exactly when $n=2$; rigidity needs $n\ge 3$ so the symmetric space has rank-1 with no continuous deformations.

questions.q: Which of the following is NOT a hypothesis of the classical Mostow rigidity theorem?
choices: $M_1, M_2$ admit hyperbolic metrics of constant sectional curvature $-1$
questions.explain: Simple connectivity would make $\pi_1$ trivial; the theorem operates on closed hyperbolic manifolds, whose universal cover $\mathbb{H}^n$ is simply connected but $M_i$ themselves have rich $\pi_1$.

questions.q: A closed hyperbolic 3-manifold is a quotient $\mathbb{H}^3/\Gamma$ where $\Gamma$ is:
choices: A torsion-free discrete cocompact subgroup of $\mathrm{PSL}_2(\mathbb{C})$
questions.explain: Closed = cocompact, smooth = torsion-free, properly discontinuous action = discrete; the orientation-preserving isometry group of $\mathbb{H}^3$ is $\mathrm{PSL}_2(\mathbb{C})$.

questions.q: Why is $\mathrm{PSL}_2(\mathbb{C})$ the right Lie group to host these lattices?
choices: It is the orientation-preserving isometry group of $\mathbb{H}^3$
It is the Galois group of $\mathbb{C}/\mathbb{R}$
questions.explain: $\mathrm{Isom}^+(\mathbb{H}^3)\cong\mathrm{PSL}_2(\mathbb{C})$ acting by Möbius transformations on the boundary $\widehat{\mathbb{C}}=S^2$.

questions.q: What is the boundary at infinity of $\mathbb{H}^3$?
questions.explain: $\mathbb{H}^3$ compactifies by adding the round sphere $S^2$ at infinity; $\mathrm{PSL}_2(\mathbb{C})$ acts on it by Möbius transformations.

questions.q: A map $f:\mathbb{H}^n\to\mathbb{H}^n$ is a $(K,C)$-quasi-isometry if for all $x,y$:
choices: $\tfrac{1}{K} d(x,y) - C \le d(f(x),f(y)) \le K\,d(x,y) + C$, with $f$ coarsely surjective
questions.explain: Quasi-isometry is the coarse multiplicative-and-additive bound — the right notion for invariants of finitely generated groups.

questions.q: Mostow's analytic centerpiece extends a $\pi_1$-equivariant quasi-isometry of $\mathbb{H}^n$ to a homeomorphism of $S^{n-1}$ that is:
questions.explain: The boundary extension is quasi-conformal — controlled distortion of infinitesimal balls — even when the bulk map is far from smooth.

questions.q: Why does the lift of a homotopy equivalence $M_1\to M_2$ between closed hyperbolic manifolds become a quasi-isometry of universal covers?
choices: Because every continuous map is a quasi-isometry
Because Švarc–Milnor: a cocompact properly discontinuous isometric action makes $\Gamma$ quasi-isometric to its space, and the lift is $\Gamma$-equivariant Lipschitz
questions.explain: Švarc–Milnor: if $\Gamma$ acts cocompactly and properly discontinuously on a proper geodesic space, the orbit map is a quasi-isometry. The lift inherits this.

questions.q: A measurable action of a group $\Gamma$ on $(X,\mu)$ is ergodic if:
choices: Every $\Gamma$-invariant measurable subset has measure $0$ or full measure
questions.explain: Ergodicity = no measurable invariant subsets except null/conull — the measure-theoretic version of irreducibility.

questions.q: In Mostow's proof, ergodicity of $\Gamma$ on the boundary is used to show that the quasi-conformal boundary map:
choices: Has zero distortion almost everywhere, hence is conformal (Möbius)
questions.explain: The dilatation tensor of the boundary map is $\Gamma$-invariant; ergodicity forces it constant a.e., and analysis of quasi-conformal maps then forces zero distortion → Möbius.

questions.q: Which classical result underpins ergodicity of a cocompact lattice acting on the boundary sphere?
choices: The Hopf ergodicity theorem for geodesic flow on a hyperbolic manifold
questions.explain: Hopf's argument shows the geodesic flow on a closed hyperbolic manifold is ergodic; this descends to ergodicity of $\Gamma$ on $S^{n-1}\times S^{n-1}$.

questions.q: Margulis's arithmeticity theorem states: every irreducible lattice in a connected semisimple Lie group $G$ of real rank $\ge 2$ is:
choices: Arithmetic — commensurable with the integer points of an algebraic $\mathbb{Q}$-group
questions.explain: Higher-rank lattices are forced to be number-theoretic objects; the theorem stitches differential geometry to arithmetic.

questions.q: In what crucial way does Margulis go beyond Mostow?
choices: It reduces to the rank-one case
It applies in higher rank ($\ge 2$) and produces an arithmetic structure, not merely an isometry
questions.explain: Mostow handles rank one (including $\mathbb{H}^n$) producing isometric uniqueness; Margulis attacks higher rank and yields arithmeticity — a stronger structural conclusion.

questions.q: Why does $\mathbb{H}^n$ not fall under arithmeticity automatically (so non-arithmetic hyperbolic lattices can exist)?
choices: $\mathrm{Isom}(\mathbb{H}^n)$ has real rank $1$, below the threshold of Margulis's theorem
questions.explain: Real rank of $\mathrm{Isom}(\mathbb{H}^n)$ is $1$; Gromov–Piatetski-Shapiro famously construct non-arithmetic hyperbolic lattices in every dimension.

questions.q: A consequence of Mostow rigidity for closed hyperbolic 3-manifolds is:
choices: Volume is unbounded in any homotopy class
Volume depends on the choice of metric
questions.explain: Since the metric is determined by topology, every metric invariant — volume, length spectrum, Laplace spectrum — becomes a topological invariant.

questions.q: Gromov's simplicial volume $\|M\|$ of a closed hyperbolic $n$-manifold satisfies:
choices: $\|M\| = \mathrm{Vol}(M)/v_n$, where $v_n$ is the volume of a regular ideal hyperbolic $n$-simplex
questions.explain: Gromov–Thurston: simplicial volume equals hyperbolic volume divided by the maximum-volume ideal simplex constant; the ratio is rigid.

questions.q: Where does Mostow rigidity fit in Thurston's geometrisation programme for 3-manifolds?
choices: It rules out hyperbolic geometry from the eight Thurston model geometries
It guarantees that the hyperbolic pieces of the JSJ–geometric decomposition are unique up to isometry
It implies all 3-manifolds admit a hyperbolic metric
questions.explain: Geometrisation cuts a 3-manifold into pieces each modelled on one of eight homogeneous geometries; the hyperbolic pieces are rigid by Mostow.
